questions.question: Which tool produces the error `fatal error: stdio.h: No such file or directory`?
questions.explanation: Header inclusion (`#include`) is the preprocessor's job. A missing header is a preprocessor error. Fix: check the include path (`-I`) and that the header is installed.

questions.question: Which tool produces the error `undefined reference to 'printf'`?
questions.explanation: 'Undefined reference' is the linker's signature error: a symbol was declared and used but no `.o` or library passed to the linker actually defines it. Fix: add the missing `.o` / `-l<libname>` to the link line, or check for a typo in the symbol name.

questions.question: What's the difference between a static (`.a`) and a shared (`.so` / `.dylib`) library at link time?
options: The static library's code is copied into the executable; the shared library is referenced and loaded at runtime
Static libraries can't have multiple files
Shared libraries are slower at runtime
questions.explanation: Static linking copies the needed object code from `libfoo.a` into the executable, producing a self-contained binary. Shared linking records a reference to `libfoo.so`; at process startup the dynamic loader maps the `.so` into the process address space. Same source, different deployment trade-off.

questions.question: Why does `gcc -shared` require `-fPIC` on the source files?
options: Shared libraries can be loaded at any address; PIC (position-independent code) generates references that don't depend on a fixed load address
`-fPIC` is a marketing flag for Intel
questions.explanation: A shared library may be mapped at a different virtual address in every process that uses it. Position-independent code uses relative addressing for global symbols, so the same library binary works regardless of its load address. Without `-fPIC`, the linker would refuse (or produce text relocations, a deprecated workaround).

questions.question: In a Makefile, what's the difference between `$<` and `$^`?
options: `$<` is the target, `$^` is the prerequisites
`$<` is the first prerequisite, `$^` is all prerequisites
`$<` is the output file, `$^` is the input file
questions.explanation: In a pattern rule like `%.o: %.c headers.h`, `$<` is the first prereq (`%.c`), `$^` is the full list (`%.c headers.h`). Use `$<` when you only want the source file (compile rule); use `$^` when you want everything (link rule).

questions.question: You edit `greet.h` and re-run `make`. With a hand-written Makefile (no `-MMD` dependency tracking), what happens?
options: Every `.c` file rebuilds because `make` recompiles on any change
Nothing rebuilds, because `greet.h` isn't listed in any rule's prerequisites — and stale binaries result
questions.explanation: Make only knows about prerequisites you list. If `greet.h` isn't a prereq of `main.o`, editing the header doesn't trigger `main.o`'s rebuild — and you get stale code in the binary. The fix is auto-generated dependency files (`-MMD -MP`) that include every header each `.c` actually included, so make knows the real graph.
